title: Winter Paralympic Games

infobox.value: | * [Asian Para Games](/wiki/Asian_Para_Games "Asian Para Games") * [African Para Games](/wiki/African_Para_Games "African Para Games") * [European Para Championships](/wiki/European_Para_Championships "European Para Championships") * [Parapan American Games](/wiki/Parapan_American_Games "Parapan American Games")

(officially known as **Paralympic Winter Games**) is an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") where [athletes with physical, visual, and intellectual disabilities](/wiki/Parasports "Parasports") compete in [snow and ice sports](/wiki/Winter_sports "Winter sports"). The event includes athletes with [mobility impairments](/wiki/Mobility_impairment "Mobility impairment"), [amputations](/wiki/Amputation "Amputation"), [blindness](/wiki/Blindness "Blindness"), and [cerebral palsy](/wiki/Cerebral_palsy "Cerebral palsy"). The Paralympic Winter Games are held every four years directly following the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") and (since 1992) hosted in the same city. The [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") (IPC) oversees the Games. Medals are awarded in each event: with gold for first place, silver for second, and bronze for third, following the tradition that the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") began in 1904.

The **Winter Paralympic Games** (officially known as **Paralympic Winter Games**) is an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") where [athletes with physical, visual, and intellectual disabilities](/wiki/Parasports "Parasports") compete in [snow and ice sports](/wiki/Winter_sports "Winter sports"). The event includes athletes with [mobility impairments](/wiki/Mobility_impairment "Mobility impairment"), [amputations](/wiki/Amputation "Amputation"), [blindness](/wiki/Blindness "Blindness"), and [cerebral palsy](/wiki/Cerebral_palsy "Cerebral palsy"). The Paralympic Winter Games are held every four years directly following the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") and (since 1992) hosted in the same city. The [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") (IPC) oversees the Games. Medals are awarded in each event: with gold for first place, silver for second, and bronze for third, following the tradition that the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") began in 1904.

The Winter Paralympics began in [1976](/wiki/1976_Winter_Paralympics "1976 Winter Paralympics") in [Örnsköldsvik](/wiki/%C3%96rnsk%C3%B6ldsvik "Örnsköldsvik"), Sweden. Those Games were the first Paralympics that featured athletes other than those in wheelchairs. The Games have expanded and grown, including the [Summer Paralympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Paralympic_Games "Summer Paralympic Games"), to become part of the largest international sporting event after the Olympics. Given their expansion, the need for a very specific [classification system](/wiki/Disability_sport_classification "Disability sport classification") has arisen. This system has also given rise to controversy and opened the door for various forms of cheating, which has tainted the integrity of the Games.

Norway has been the top-ranking (medals) nation for four Paralympic Winter Games: [1980](/wiki/1980_Winter_Paralympics "1980 Winter Paralympics"), [1988](/wiki/1988_Winter_Paralympics "1988 Winter Paralympics"), [1994](/wiki/1994_Winter_Paralympics "1994 Winter Paralympics"), and [1998](/wiki/1998_Winter_Paralympics "1998 Winter Paralympics"). Germany has been the top-ranking (medals) for three Paralympic Winter Games: [1976](/wiki/1976_Winter_Paralympics "1976 Winter Paralympics"), [2002](/wiki/2002_Winter_Paralympics "2002 Winter Paralympics"), and [2010](/wiki/2010_Winter_Paralympics "2010 Winter Paralympics"). China ([2022](/wiki/2022_Winter_Paralympics "2022 Winter Paralympics") and [2026](/wiki/2026_Winter_Paralympics "2026 Winter Paralympics")), Russia ([2006](/wiki/2006_Winter_Paralympics "2006 Winter Paralympics") and [2014](/wiki/2014_Winter_Paralympics "2014 Winter Paralympics")) and the United States ([1992](/wiki/1992_Winter_Paralympics "1992 Winter Paralympics") and [2018](/wiki/2018_Winter_Paralympics "2018 Winter Paralympics")) have been the top-ranking nation two time each. Austria ([1984](/wiki/1984_Winter_Paralympics "1984 Winter Paralympics")) has been the top-ranking nation one time.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Paralympic_Games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The origins of the Winter Paralympics are much similar to the [Summer Paralympics](/wiki/Summer_Paralympics "Summer Paralympics"). Injured soldiers returning from World War II sought sports as an avenue to healing.[[2]](#cite_note-phistory-2) Organized by Dr. [Ludwig Guttmann](/wiki/Ludwig_Guttmann "Ludwig Guttmann"), sports competitions between British convalescent hospitals began in 1948 and continued until 1960 when a parallel Olympics was held in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") after the [1960 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics"). Over 400 wheelchair athletes competed at the [1960 Paralympic Games](/wiki/1960_Paralympic_Games "1960 Paralympic Games"), which became known as the first Paralympics.[[2]](#cite_note-phistory-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/1stWinterParaolympics1976.jpg/250px-1stWinterParaolympics1976.jpg)](/wiki/File:1stWinterParaolympics1976.jpg)

Official sticker from the first Winter Paralympics held in [Örnsköldsvik](/wiki/%C3%96rnsk%C3%B6ldsvik "Örnsköldsvik"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), 1976

Sepp Zwicknagl, a pioneer of snow sports for disabled athletes, was a double-leg amputee Austrian skier who experimented skiing using prosthetics. His work helped pioneer [technological advances](/wiki/Technological_changes_at_the_Paralympic_Games "Technological changes at the Paralympic Games") for people with disabilities who wished to participate in winter sports.[[3]](#cite_note-origins-3) Advances were slow, and it was not until 1974 that the first official world ski competition for physically impaired athletes, featuring downhill and a cross-country skiing, was held.[[3]](#cite_note-origins-3) The first Winter Paralympics were held in [1976](/wiki/1976_Winter_Paralympics "1976 Winter Paralympics") at [Örnsköldsvik](/wiki/%C3%96rnsk%C3%B6ldsvik "Örnsköldsvik"), Sweden from February 21–28. Alpine and Nordic skiing for amputees and visually impaired athletes were the main events but ice sledge racing was included as a demonstration event.[[3]](#cite_note-origins-3) There were 198 participating athletes from 16 countries,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and it was the first time that athletes with impairments other than wheelchair athletes were permitted to compete.[[5]](#cite_note-history-5)

Starting in [1988](/wiki/1988_Summer_Paralympics "1988 Summer Paralympics") the Summer Paralympics were held in the same host city as the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games"). This was due to an agreement reached between the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) and the [International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") (IPC). The [1992 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Paralympics "1992 Winter Paralympics") were the first Winter Games to use the same facilities as the [Winter Olympics](/wiki/1992_Winter_Olympics "1992 Winter Olympics").[[5]](#cite_note-history-5)

Sportspeople with intellectual disabilities competed at the Winter Paralympics for the first time in [1992](/wiki/1992_Winter_Paralympics "1992 Winter Paralympics") in alpine skiing and cross country skiing. These were demonstration events. For the [1994 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Paralympics "1994 Winter Paralympics"), biathlon was added as a demonstration sport. Athletes with intellectual disabilities have not competed at a Winter Paralympics since [1998](/wiki/1998_Winter_Paralympics "1998 Winter Paralympics").[[6]](#cite_note-:1-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Cheating

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Paralympic_Games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cheating")]

Main article: [Cheating at the Paralympic Games](/wiki/Cheating_at_the_Paralympic_Games "Cheating at the Paralympic Games")

Athletes have cheated by over-representing impairment to have a competitive advantage, and the use of performance-enhancing drugs.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) German skier Thomas Oelsner became the first Winter Paralympian to test positive for steroids in [2002](/wiki/2002_Winter_Paralympics "2002 Winter Paralympics"). He had won two gold medals in the alpine events but was stripped of his medals.[[10]](#cite_note-10) One concern now facing Paralympic officials is the technique of boosting blood pressure, known as [autonomic dysreflexia](/wiki/Autonomic_dysreflexia "Autonomic dysreflexia"). The increase in blood pressure can improve performance by 15% and is most effective in the endurance sports such as [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Paralympic_cross-country_skiing "Paralympic cross-country skiing"). To increase blood pressure athletes will deliberately cause trauma to limbs below a spinal injury. This trauma can include breaking bones, strapping extremities in too tightly and using high-pressured compression stockings. The injury is painless to the athlete but affects the body and impacts the athlete's blood pressure, as can techniques like allowing the bladder to overfill.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

[International Paralympic Committee](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") (IPC) found evidence that the [Disappearing Positive Methodology](/wiki/Doping_in_Russia "Doping in Russia") was in operation at the [2014 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/2014_Winter_Paralympics "2014 Winter Paralympics") in Sochi.[[12]](#cite_note-IPC160807-12) On 7 August 2016, the IPC's Governing Board voted unanimously to ban the entire Russian team from the [2016 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/2016_Summer_Paralympics "2016 Summer Paralympics"), citing the Russian Paralympic Committee's inability to enforce the IPC's Anti-Doping Code and the World Anti-Doping Code which is "a fundamental constitutional requirement".[[12]](#cite_note-IPC160807-12) IPC President Sir Philip Craven stated that the Russian government had "catastrophically failed its Para athletes".[[13]](#cite_note-Craven160807-13) IPC Athletes' Council Chairperson Todd Nicholson said that Russia had used athletes as "pawns" in order to "show global prowess".[[14]](#cite_note-Nicholson160807-14)

## Disability categories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Paralympic_Games&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Disability categories")]

The IPC has established six disability categories applying to both the Summer and Winter Paralympics. Athletes with one of these physical disabilities are able to compete in the Paralympics, though not every sport can allow for every disability category.[[15]](#cite_note-categories-15)

* **Amputee**: Athletes with a [partial or total loss](/wiki/Amputation "Amputation") of at least one [limb](/wiki/Limb_(anatomy) "Limb (anatomy)").
* **Cerebral Palsy**: Athletes with non-progressive [brain damage](/wiki/Brain_damage "Brain damage"), for example [cerebral palsy](/wiki/Cerebral_palsy "Cerebral palsy"), [traumatic brain injury](/wiki/Traumatic_brain_injury "Traumatic brain injury"), [stroke](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") or similar disabilities affecting muscle control, [balance](/wiki/Sense_of_balance "Sense of balance") or [coordination](/wiki/Coordination_(physiology) "Coordination (physiology)").
* **Intellectual Disability**: Athletes with a significant [impairment in intellectual functioning](/wiki/Developmental_disability "Developmental disability") and associated limitations in adaptive behavior.
* **Wheelchair**: Athletes with [spinal cord injuries](/wiki/Spinal_cord_injury "Spinal cord injury") and other disabilities which require them to compete in a [wheelchair](/wiki/Wheelchair "Wheelchair").
* **Visually Impaired**: Athletes with [vision impairment](/wiki/Visual_impairment "Visual impairment") ranging from partial vision, sufficient to be judged [legally blind](/wiki/Legal_blindness "Legal blindness"), to total [blindness](/wiki/Blindness "Blindness").
* ***Les Autres***: Athletes with a physical disability that does not fall strictly under one of the other five categories, such as [dwarfism](/wiki/Dwarfism "Dwarfism"), [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis") or [congenital](/wiki/Congenital_disorder "Congenital disorder") [deformities](/wiki/Deformity "Deformity") of the limbs such as that caused by [thalidomide](/wiki/Thalidomide "Thalidomide") (the name for this category is [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") for "the others").[[15]](#cite_note-categories-15)

## Classifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Winter_Paralympic_Games&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Classifications")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Andy_Soule%2C_2010_Paralympics.jpg/250px-Andy_Soule%2C_2010_Paralympics.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andy_Soule,_2010_Paralympics.jpg)

Paralympian [Andy Soule](/wiki/Andy_Soule "Andy Soule") in the 12.5 km cross-country race at the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics")

Within the six disability categories the athletes still need to be divided according to their level of impairment. The classification systems differ from sport to sport. The systems are designed to open up Paralympic sports to as many athletes as possible, who can participate in fair competitions against athletes with similar levels of ability. The closest equivalents in non-disabled competitions are age classifications in junior sports, and weight divisions in wrestling, boxing, and weightlifting. Classifications vary in accordance with the different skills required to perform the sport. The biggest challenge in the classification system is how to account for the wide variety and severity of disabilities. As a result, there will always be a range of impairment within a classification.[[16]](#cite_note-classdescribed-16) What follows is a list of the Winter Paralympic sports and a general description of how they are classified.

**[Alpine skiing](/wiki/Alpine_skiing_at_the_Winter_Paralympics "Alpine skiing at the Winter Paralympics")**:
There are two events in alpine skiing: slalom and giant slalom. Alpine skiing accommodates athletes with the following physical limitations: spinal injury, Cerebral Palsy, amputation, *Les Autres* and blindness/visual impairment. There are eleven classifications, seven for standing athletes, three for sitting athletes, and three for visually impaired athletes. The divisions are defined by the degree of the athletes' function and the need for assistive equipment (prosthesis, ski poles, etc.).[[17]](#cite_note-17) Snowboard Cross is technically now included in this category, though competition will take place with only limited classifications (see below).

**[Biathlon](/wiki/Paralympic_Biathlon "Paralympic Biathlon")**:
Biathlon is a combination of cross-country skiing with target shooting. It requires physical stamina and accurate shooting. The events are open to athletes with physical disabilities and visual impairments. There are fifteen classes in which athletes will be placed depending on their level of function. Twelve divisions are for athletes with a physical impairment and three divisions are for athletes with a visual impairment. The athletes compete together, and their finishing times are entered into a formula with their disability class to determine the athletes' overall finish order. Visually impaired athletes are able to compete through the use of acoustic signals. The signal intensity varies depending upon whether or not the athlete is on target.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

**[Cross-country skiing](/wiki/Paralympic_cross-country_skiing "Paralympic cross-country skiing")**:
Cross-country skiing, also known as Nordic skiing, is open to athletes with Cerebral Palsy, amputations, the need for a wheelchair, visual impairment and intellectual impairment. There are fifteen classifications, three for visually impaired athletes, nine for standing athletes and three for seated athletes. The divisions are determined in a similar fashion to alpine skiing with attention given to the athletes' level of function and need for assistive devices.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/2010ParalympicsUnitedStatesVsJapanIceSledgeHockey.jpg/250px-2010ParalympicsUnitedStatesVsJapanIceSledgeHockey.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010ParalympicsUnitedStatesVsJapanIceSledgeHockey.jpg)

Ice sledge hockey game at the [2010 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Paralympics "2010 Winter Paralympics")

**[Ice Sledge Hockey](/wiki/Ice_sledge_hockey_at_the_Winter_Paralympics "Ice sledge hockey at the Winter Paralympics")**:
Ice sledge hockey is open only to competitors with a physical disability in the lower part of their body. Since 2010 it has been a mixed-gender sport at the Winter Paralympics, however participation of female athletes remains low [[20]](#cite_note-20)The game is played using international hockey rules with some modifications. Athletes sit on sledges with two blades that allow the puck to go beneath the sledge. They also use two sticks, which have a spike-end for pushing and a blade-end for shooting. The athletes are classified into three groups: group 1 is for athletes with no sitting balance or with major impairment in both upper and lower limbs, group 2 is for athletes with some sitting balance and moderate impairment in their extremities and athletes in group 3 have good balance and mild impairment in their upper and lower limbs.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

**[Wheelchair curling](/wiki/Wheelchair_curling_at_the_Winter_Paralympics "Wheelchair curling at the Winter Paralympics")**:
Wheelchair curling is a coed team event for athletes with permanent lower limb disabilities that require them to use a wheelchair in their daily lives. Athletes with Cerebral Palsy or Multiple Sclerosis can also play if they use a wheelchair. Delivery of the stone can be by hand release or the use of a pole. There are no classifications in this event except the requirement that all athletes participating must have need for a wheelchair for daily mobility.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

**[Para snowboard](/wiki/Para_snowboard "Para snowboard")**:
On 2 May 2012, the International Paralympic Committee (IPC) officially sanctioned Para snowboarding (formerly known as *adaptive snowboarding*) as a medal event in the 2014 Winter Paralympic Games under Alpine Skiing. There were men's and women's standing [snowboard cross](/wiki/Snowboard_cross "Snowboard cross") competitions.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The sport's Paralympic debut featured events for only athletes with overall lower-limb impairments. Athletes with amputations were permitted to wear a prosthesis. The events were held in a [time trial](/wiki/Time_trial "Time trial") format, but no accommodation was made based on an athlete's level of impairment. At the [2018 Winter Paralympics](/wiki/2018_Winter_Paralympics "2018 Winter Paralympics"), the number of events increased from 2 to 10 with the split of the lower limb classifications into two sub-groups (LL-1 for greater impairment and LL-2 for lesser impairment), and the addition of the [banked slalom](/wiki/Banked_slalom "Banked slalom"). The snowboard cross event was run in a head-to-head format with multiple knockout heats to determine the medal winners.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In 2022, events for athletes with upper-limb impairments were added, but the [IPC](/wiki/International_Paralympic_Committee "International Paralympic Committee") eliminated the LL-1 category for women due to a dearth of athletes in that class. Those athletes were initially barred from competing at all until [Brenna Huckaby](/wiki/Brenna_Huckaby "Brenna Huckaby") of the United States prevailed in a lawsuit to "compete up" against the LL-2 class.
